content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 9, 2000


Elfrieda Hall
Chairperson, Project Area Committee
City of Indio Redevelopment Agency
100 Civic Center Mall
P.O. Drawer 1788
Indio, California 92202

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-99-288

Dear Ms. Hall:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Lawrence M. Chank,
member of the City of Indio Redevelopment Agency ("Agency") regarding the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ The Act requires the Commission to
provide written advice to any person whose duties under the Act are in question or to that
person's authorized representative. (Section 83114(b); Regulation 18329(b)(1).) However, the
Commission does not respond to requests where all the material facts have not been provided, to
requests hypothetical in nature, or to requests relating to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(C); (b)(8)(D); (b)(8)(A).) Since your request does not include material facts needed
by us to fully assess Mr. Chank's situation, we are unable to provide you with formal written
advice.² However, in order to provide you with some guidance, we have included additional
information on the applicable law.

I. QUESTION

Under what circumstances will a loan from the Agency to Mr. Chank result in his
disqualification concerning Agency decisions?

________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Please note that only formal written advice confers the immunity provided under Section 83114(b) and
Regulations 18329(b)(7). Informal assistance does not provide immunity. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-99-288
Page 2

II. CONCLUSION

Because Mr. Chank's loan from the Agency most likely constitutes income, the Agency
would be considered a source of income in which he has an economic interest. Consequently, he
will be precluded from participating in any governmental decisions where a material financial
effect on the Agency is reasonably foreseeable to result, unless the "public generally" exception
applies.

III. FACTS

According to your correspondence and phone conversations with FPPC staff counsel, you
are the Chairperson of the Project Area Committee ("PAC") of the City of Indio Redevelopment
Agency and are the authorized representative of Lawrence M. Chank. You are writing to request
advice about Mr. Chank's involvement as a member of the PAC which is currently in the process
of establishing a new project area. Mr. Chank owns property within an existing project area
located at 81-725 Highway 111 in Indio. He acquired this property pursuant to a July 5, 1995,
Disposition and Development Agreement (the "DDA") with the Indio Redevelopment Agency.

The DDA provides that Mr. Chank's obligation to pay for the property is secured by a
promissory note given to the Agency. In addition, the DDA recites that the agreement with
Mr. Chank is based on his particular qualifications and identity. The note also secures an
additional loan which the Agency made to Mr. Chank for the development of the property. Over
$10,000 of the principal of the loan is still outstanding. Mr. Chank discloses this loan on
Schedule D of his Statement of Economic Interests.

IV. ANALYSIS

The primary purpose for the conflict of interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that
public officials, whether elected or appointed, perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from bias caused by their own financial interests. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this
goal, Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.

Section 87103 of the Act provides that an official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the following:

"(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or
indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

File No. I-99-288
Page 3

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.
(e) Any donor of, donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a
gift or gifts aggregating [$300] or more in value provided to,
received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made." (Section 87103.)

Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under Section 87100 is an eight-step
process³:

1. Is Mr. Chank a "public official" within the meaning of the rules?

In the In re Rotman opinion, the Commission found that members of a project area
committee are subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions since a project area committee is
considered to have decisionmaking authority and, therefore, is not solely advisory. (In re
Rotman (1987) 10 FPPC Ops. 1.) Consequently, Mr. Chank, as a member of the Project Area
Committee of the City of Indio Redevelopment Agency, is a public official under the Act and is
subject to the prohibition specified in Section 87100.

2. Is Mr. Chank making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

Without material facts regarding a governmental decision, we are unable to conduct a
formal analysis of this question. However, your PAC undoubtedly is set up in accordance with a
governing authority to carry out specified objectives and undertake certain governmental actions.
For this reason, Mr. Chank's participation with the PAC most likely would constitute "making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision."

3. What are Mr. Chank's economic interests?

In the Stausboll Advice Letter, No. I-99-128, we concluded that the language given at
Section 87103(c) is the full and complete definition of "source of income" for purposes of
determining whether a particular loan is income, an economic interest under the Act's conflict of

³ See FPPC document entitled "Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overview," June 1999.

File No. 1-99-288
Page 4

interest provisions. As a result, for a loan NOT to be considered income, the loan must meet
each of the following criteria:

1) the loan must have been made by a commercial lending institution
2) the loan must have been made in the regular course of business
3) the terms of the loan must have been available to the public
4) the terms of the loan must have been made without regard to official status

The terms of the loan appear to fail the third criterion of being "available to the public"
since your correspondence indicated that the loan was based on Mr. Chank's "particular
qualifications and identity." If the terms of the loan were available only to Mr. Chank, then the
loan does not meet this specified criteria and will be characterized as income. Alternatively, if
Mr. Chank was given the terms of the loan due to his "official status" as a member of the PAC,
then the loan will also fail the last criterion and be considered income under these conditions as
well. In either circumstance, Mr. Chank's loan will be construed as income, creating for him an
economic interest in the Agency.

4. Is Mr. Chank's economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision?

Your letter did not specify any prospective governmental decision. Therefore, we cannot
dispositively complete this analysis. However, Mr. Chank's situation differs from the typical
project area committee member in such a way as to create a greater potential for a conflict of
interest. In most cases, a member of a project area committee merely has an interest in real estate
or a business located in the project area. In contrast, Mr. Chank most likely has an economic
interest in the Agency itself. For this reason, it is likely that Mr. Chank's economic interest (i.e.,
the Redevelopment Agency) will be directly involved in most governmental decisions by the
PAC.

5. What kinds of financial impact on Mr. Chank's economic interests are considered
material or important enough to trigger a conflict of interest?

Under Regulation 18705.3(a), any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person
who is a source of income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before
the official's agency, is deemed material. Consequently, any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on the Agency as a result of a governmental decision by the PAC is material, assuming it
is directly involved in the decision.

6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will result in the materiality
standard being met for Mr. Chank's economic interest?

Without having information on a specific governmental decision, we cannot thoroughly
conduct this analysis. However, many governmental decisions by the PAC will likely result in
some financial effect on the Agency. Because any financial effect on the Agency is material, it is

File No. I-99-288
Page 5

reasonably foreseeable that this materiality standard almost always will be met for Mr. Chank's
economic interest.

7. Does the "public generally" exception apply to Mr. Chank's situation?

Under the "public generally" exception provided by Section 87103 and Regulation
18707, even if Mr. Chank has a conflict of interest, he may not be disqualified from participating
in a governmental decision where the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his
economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally. This exception is
applicable if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect a "significant segment" of
the public "in substantially the same manner" as it affects the public official's economic interest.
(Regulation 18707(b)(1) & (b)(2).) In the absence of additional information, we are unable to
determine whether the "public generally" exception will apply to Mr. Chank's situation.

8. Is Mr. Chank's participation legally required?

Section 87101 and Regulation 18708 provide that a public official is not legally required
to make or participate in the making of a governmental decision unless there exists no alternative
source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision.
It is not apparent from your letter that this exception applies.

Enclosed for your information is an overview pertaining to the conflict of interests
provisions under the Act. We hope this information will be useful to you.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: [signature] Natalie Bocanegra
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official

12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,

management.

interest provisions. As a result, for a loan NOT to be considered income, the loan must meet

CONCLUSION: Because Mr. Chank’s loan from the Agency most likely constitutes income, the Agency

will be precluded from participating in any governmental decisions where a material financial
effect on the Agency is reasonably foreseeable to result, unless the “public generally” exception
applies.
